title: Lesson 204
transcript: Let's talk about malware. What is malware? Let's start with that simple definition. Malware is software that's been intentionally written to somehow harm your system, do something nefarious. Now, we often misuse some of the terminology around this. We tend to call everything a virus, for instance. But a virus is actually a particular type of malware. So what I'm gonna do next in this next few minutes is talk about all the different types of malware that are out there. And I'm gonna give you very specific, very concise definitions for each of the different types of malware. because it's not uncommon on the exam that you'll get questions about the different types of malware. And so it's important for you to understand exactly what they look like, what do they do, and One other caveat I'll give to this as well, and that is that malware out in the wild often exhibits multiple different aspects or different types of malware that we're going to talk about here. That'll become obvious as we talk about it. So let's start with the most commonly referred to piece of malware, a virus. What is the defining characteristic of a virus? It's actually a piece of malware that needs to be triggered by a user. That's what we call a virus. It's a piece of malware that somehow has to be triggered by the user. Which implies something really important. What's one of the best ways of preventing a virus outbreak on a system? It's actually user training and awareness so they know not to double click on particular types of files and open things up. That's the best way of preventing a virus. Next, let's look at worms. Worms are particularly dangerous because worms are self-propagating. They can spread on their own. How? Well, they spread through some known vulnerability that the malware writer has found. And so they write this of the way to exploit this vulnerability into their worm. And then that worm can self-propagate, can spread on its own. And that's why when you've heard of major malware outbreaks in the past, things like SQL Slammer, the reason they were able to spread so incredibly quickly to millions of systems in a matter of hours is because they were a worm. They were self-propagating. Next, let's look at companions. Companions malware is not actually doing anything nefarious on its own. Instead, a companion is just a helper. It's helping out the major piece of malware in some way. A perfect example of a companion is something like a packer that You know, helps condense down the malware code and then de-pack it, un-condense it later on. So that's a companion. Doesn't do anything evil on its own. Next, let's look at macros. When you hear the word macro, you probably think of the macros that you build into say Excel documents to automate certain tasks. That's a perfect example of what a macro virus is or a macro malware. A macro is code that we've written into another application. And the Microsoft products where you can use VBScript to write macros is a perfect example of macro malware. You can actually use VBScript to write malicious code into a macro that you then add into something like an Excel document. Which is why when you open up an Excel document, something like that, in Word, you get such dire warnings. Are you really sure you want to open this? Because often, or sometimes, opening an Excel document can actually get one of these macros to run automatically and do something harmful to your system. Next, let's look at multipartite malware. The name actually does a pretty good job of explaining this, multipartite. That word multi in there, It means more than one. And that's what a multi-partite piece of malware means. It means it's spreading in more than one different way. So a perfect example of a multi-partite piece of malware is actually Stuxnet. If you've ever read into this a little bit or heard about it, Stuxnet was actually a piece of malware that was designed to be installed and run in an Iranian nuclear refinement facility where they were creating plutonium. In order to create plutonium, you have to spin centrifuges at really, really high speed, like 40,000 RPM. This equipment is obviously very sensitive. So someone, the US, wrote a piece of malware to run on this Iranian facility. And what it was doing was actually spinning these centrifuges just a little bit too fast to cause them to fail. And even more deviously, it was actually hiding what it was doing from the operators of this facility. Their systems, their screens, their dashboards were telling them everything's working perfectly, and yet their equipment was failing. Now, as you can imagine, this was a very well-protected facility. It was certainly not connected to the internet. So how did this malware actually get into this facility? And Stuxnet was running in real facilities. So how did it get in there? Well, it's suspected that the first way that it got in was on a USB stick. So the first way that Stuxnet infected a system was through a USB vulnerability. And then, once it was on the first system, it then spread across the network. So it spread in multiple different ways. First through a USB vulnerability, and then through a network vulnerability. Multi-partite. Next, polymorphic. When you hear those words polymorphic or that word polymorphic, it's actually composed of two different words, poly and morphic. Poly means many, morphic means to change. And that's a really good definition of what polymorphic malware is. It's constantly changing, morphing. Why? To evade detection. Most anti-malware systems are signature based. They look for a particular set of characteristics, a certain file name, file size, variable names within the malware. It looks for a specific set of characteristics. And if a piece of malware is constantly morphing, changing itself, it's to evade detection. So that's polymorphic. Next, let's look at Trojans. So you've probably heard, if you know your Greek mythology, the Trojan horse. It looked like a very lovely thing that you would want to bring inside your defenses, but it was actually filled with... something you didn't want, something evil. And that's a perfect example of a Trojan. A Trojan is something that looks very innocent, a file that you would want to install, but it's actually filled with malware. So that's a Trojan. Next, botnets. All right, there's a few pieces that we need to know about botnets. First of all, let's understand the concept of what is a botnet. Well, a botnet is this concept that an attacker finds a vulnerability that they can exploit that allows them to take over and install some software on a victim system. and they install a little bot or they make that system a zombie. And what they're doing is they're essentially allowing the attacker gets the ability to remotely control the system, to get that system to send out spam email, to do cryptocurrency mining, to do all sorts of nefarious things. And now the idea of a bot net is that we don't just have one zombie that we can remotely command and control. We have hundreds, thousands, maybe millions. And when you connect all of these systems together, you create a giant bot net that allows the attacker to remotely control potentially millions of systems. and this is frankly how the vast majority of spam gets sent on the internet is by botnets and they're used nowadays for lots of cryptocurrency mining things like that in fact you can even get botnet as a service if you look on the dark web there's some excellent services out there for botnets as a service that you can buy Next, let's look at boot sector infectors. Okay, what's a boot sector? Well, on a hard drive, you have a little bit of memory space right at the start of the hard drive that's reserved for the firmware, for the code that's meant to help that hard drive spin up for the low-level control of the hard drive. Now, this boot sector is never meant to be accessed by the operating system, by any other code, except for when the hard drive first starts up and for the normal operation of the hard drive. Now, if a piece of malware were to install itself in the boot sector, When would that malware run when the system first starts up? And the answer is, right at the very boot up of that system, this malware would be able to run then. And which of the system's security controls would be in place? none, the operating system wouldn't be running, the anti-malware system wouldn't be running. So boot sector infectors, they're able to install themselves into the boot sector of a hard drive, which makes them very difficult to detect, and it allows them to run well before any of the other security controls would even place on a system. Next, let's look at hoaxes, pranks. Hoaxes and pranks are not actually software. They're social engineering. It's where you use techniques to get someone to do something either funny or maybe malicious to their system through social engineering techniques. So pranks are just for fun, like flipping someone's screen upside down or something like that. And hoaxes are where you actually get someone to do something that's potentially bad for their system. I'll give you a great example. You can tell your parents this. If their system is starting to run a little bit slowly, just tell them to go to the command prompt. You'll have to teach them how to do that. and then get them to type in C colon star dot star, or sorry, delete C colon star dot star. So what's that going to do? Significantly increase the speed of their system. No, don't do that. It's a very bad idea. But that's a perfect example of a hoax. Next, let's talk about logic bombs. A logic bomb is a piece of malware that when a certain set of logic is met, the bomb will go off. So I'll give you a great example of this. This is a true story. So there was a very disgruntled system administrator. He worked at a small company. He'd been there for many, many years. He had control, access to, Everything. Now, over time, he was starting to get a little disgruntled. You know, these bastards, they don't pay me enough money. And so he decided to write a logic bomb. This logic bomb asked one very simple question every morning. It asked, am I still an employee? And one morning, the HR system said, No. And what did his logic bomb do? It salted the earth. This guy had access to everything, so it deleted all the SharePoint sites, all the databases, all the backups. It destroyed everything. So that's a perfect example of a logic bomb, and also why you shouldn't do it, because he's now in jail. Next, let's look at stealth malware. Stealth malware is a piece of malware that's been specifically designed to actively disguise itself, to hide, to be stealth, to not show itself on the system. Next, let's look at ransomware. I'm sure everyone has heard about ransomware now. It's in the news all the time. Ransomware is this concept of how can attackers monetize breaking into your system? The way that they do it is they'll encrypt your data or your systems, or in some other way, make your systems and data unavailable, and then they'll require some sort of bribe, some sort of ransom to unlock your systems. That's ransomware. And it's becoming incredibly pervasive nowadays because frankly, it's good money for the attackers. This is a very good revenue stream for them. Next, let's look at root kits. All right, this is very sophisticated and stealthy malware. So we've talked about the system kernel. The system kernel is the heart of the operating system. It's what controls all the low-level functionality. It's the software that controls the entire system. A rootkit is a piece of malware that's able to infect that system kernel to, in a sense, control that system kernel to give the rootkit privileged access. And with that privileged access, the rootkit can basically do whatever it wants. And one very important thing that rootkits will do is they'll disguise or hide themselves. And because they can control the operating system, if you do a search, for instance, if the anti-malware system does a scan through the hard drive to look for the rootkit, the rootkit will just tell the operating system not to display any of the files that are the rootkit, which makes rootkits very difficult to detect and remove from systems. Very dangerous malware. Next, let's look at data diddlers. And I gotta start this off by saying I didn't make these names up, okay? So what's a data diddler? A data diddler makes very small changes to your data over a long period of time. Why? to avoid detection. So if you make very, very small changes over a long period of time, it'll probably evade detection. Again, I'm not making these names up. There's one specific type of Data Diddler. It's known as a salami attack, and it's specific to financial systems. So how do you slice salami? Very thinly. That's the idea of a salami attack. The idea is when you're performing financial transactions, calculating interest, things like that, there's always some rounding that has to be done. And in a salami attack, you take off fractions of a penny from those financial calculations and you store those fractions of a penny in another account somewhere. And it's not very much money. It's a very, very small amount of money. But over a long period of time, it might equal a lot of money. Go and watch Office Space if you want to learn more about that one. There you go, that's the different types of malware. Now there's one final very important concept that we have to talk about related to malware, and that is this concept of zero day. So what is zero day? Well, a zero day is any of the types of malware that we've just talked about, It's when is it first released? When is it first known about? And day zero, the first day that it's released into the wild is known as a zero-day piece of malware. And the reason this is particularly dangerous, zero days, is because none of us know what these things are. We don't know what they can do. We don't know how to detect them. And so zero day malware, first time it's ever seen in the wild, very dangerous, can have a huge impact on systems and therefore very valuable pieces of malware. So there you go, that's all the pieces of malware that you need to know about.
